title: History-based regression isolation

canonicalExplanation.summary: Bisect performs a binary search over an ordered commit ancestry using a reliable good/bad test to identify the first commit where behavior changes.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A deterministic regression script marks release v1 good and current main bad, then classifies midpoint commits until one introducing commit remains. Your goal is to locate a seeded regression with bisect and verify the causative patch. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Dependencies and reproducible builds; Commits and history; Log, show and graph; Build and task commands. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Bisect performs a binary search over an ordered commit ancestry using a reliable good/bad test to identify the first commit where behavior changes. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Flaky tests, merge topology, unbuildable commits, environmental drift, and an incorrect known-good point can produce a misleading culprit. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Bisect examines code changes automatically and determines which developer caused a bug without a reproducible test.

includes: Use a reproducible good/bad test and binary search over commits to find a regression introduction.
Locate a seeded regression with bisect and verify the causative patch.
excludes: Flaky tests, merge topology, unbuildable commits, environmental drift, and an incorrect known-good point can produce a misleading culprit.

outcomes: Locate a seeded regression with bisect and verify the causative patch.

examples.title: History-based regression isolation: direct case
examples.situation: A deterministic regression script marks release v1 good and current main bad, then classifies midpoint commits until one introducing commit remains.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A deterministic regression script marks release v1 good and current main bad, then classifies midpoint commits until one introducing commit remains.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Provide trustworthy boundary commits and a deterministic classifier, then inspect and verify the identified commit as evidence rather than blame.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Provide trustworthy boundary commits and a deterministic classifier, then inspect and verify the identified commit as evidence rather than blame.

examples.title: History-based regression isolation: boundary case
examples.situation: An untestable midpoint is skipped with a recorded reason rather than guessed good or bad.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: An untestable midpoint is skipped with a recorded reason rather than guessed good or bad.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Flaky tests, merge topology, unbuildable commits, environmental drift, and an incorrect known-good point can produce a misleading culprit.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Bisect examines code changes automatically and determines which developer caused a bug without a reproducible test.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Locate a seeded regression with bisect and verify the causative patch.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Bisect examines code changes automatically and determines which developer caused a bug without a reproducible test.
misconceptions.correction: Provide trustworthy boundary commits and a deterministic classifier, then inspect and verify the identified commit as evidence rather than blame.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Bisect examines code changes automatically and determines which developer caused a bug without a reproducible test.

analogy.example: A controlled investigation can model History-based regression isolation: preserve evidence, state a hypothesis, inspect one clue, automate repeatable checks, and narrow the change point.
usefulFor: Linking debugging, static checks, history search, and automated local gates
limitations: Debuggers and hooks can alter timing or execute code, and tool findings remain bounded evidence rather than proof.
Flaky tests, merge topology, unbuildable commits, environmental drift, and an incorrect known-good point can produce a misleading culprit.

recap.summary: You should now be able to locate a seeded regression with bisect and verify the causative patch. Keep this boundary in view: Flaky tests, merge topology, unbuildable commits, environmental drift, and an incorrect known-good point can produce a misleading culprit.
retrievalPrompts: Explain History-based regression isolation without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Bisect examines code changes automatically and determines which developer caused a bug without a reproducible test.
Apply the model to this case from memory: An untestable midpoint is skipped with a recorded reason rather than guessed good or bad.
